title: TDIU Explained
desc: Getting paid at the 100% rate without a 100% schedular rating -- eligibility, evidence, and strategy

sections.heading: :star: What Is TDIU?
sections.body:
TDIU pays you at the **100% rate** even if your combined rating is less than 100%. It's the benefit most veterans don't know they qualify for.

Under `38 CFR 4.16`, TDIU exists because two veterans with the same 70% combined rating can have **vastly different functional capacities** -- one might work full-time while another can't hold any job.

> [!important] TDIU is for veterans whose service-connected disabilities prevent them from securing or following **"substantially gainful employment."** It's not about whether you can do any job in theory -- it's about whether your specific disabilities, education, work history, and training allow you to **actually earn a living**.

> [!check] If you're rated less than 100% but can't work because of your service-connected conditions, TDIU is your path to 100% compensation. Don't leave this money on the table.

sections.heading: :chart: Schedular Eligibility (38 CFR 4.16(a))
sections.body:
There are **two schedular thresholds** you need to meet. Hit either one and you're eligible.

| Threshold | Requirement |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Single disability** | One service-connected disability rated at **60% or higher** |
| **Combined** | Combined rating of **70% or higher** with at least one disability at **40% or more** |

> [!important] For the "one disability at 60%" or "one at 40%" threshold, certain disabilities are **treated as a single disability**:
1. Disabilities of one or both **upper extremities**, or one or both **lower extremities** (including bilateral factor)
2. Disabilities from a **common etiology or single accident**
3. Disabilities affecting a **single body system** (e.g., all orthopedic, all neuropsychiatric)
4. Multiple injuries **incurred in action**
5. Multiple disabilities incurred as a **prisoner of war**

> [!quote] Example: You have a 30% right knee and 20% left knee from the same injury. They combine to a single **44% disability** (with bilateral factor) for TDIU threshold purposes. That meets the 40% single-disability requirement.

> [!tip] Run the grouping math before assuming you don't qualify. Many veterans who think they're short on thresholds actually meet them when disabilities are properly grouped.

sections.heading: :shield: Extraschedular TDIU (38 CFR 4.16(b))
sections.body:
Don't meet the schedular thresholds? **You can still get TDIU** through the extraschedular path.

Under `38 CFR 4.16(b)`, the regulation is clear: "all veterans who are unable to secure and follow a substantially gainful occupation by reason of service-connected disabilities **shall be rated totally disabled**." The rating board refers these cases to the **Director of Compensation Service** for extraschedular consideration.

> [!warning] Extraschedular TDIU is **harder to get** but not impossible. The evidence needs to be strong -- you can't just file the form and hope.

**What you need for extraschedular TDIU:**
- A **compelling vocational expert opinion** explaining why you can't work
- Clear **employment history** showing inability to maintain work
- **Medical evidence** documenting functional limitations that prevent employment
- Evidence that your situation is **exceptional** -- beyond what the rating schedule contemplates

> [!check] If your disabilities prevent you from working but you don't meet the schedular numbers, file anyway. VA is **required** to refer your case for extraschedular consideration if the evidence supports unemployability.

sections.heading: :brain: What 'Substantially Gainful Employment' Means
sections.body:
This is where most veterans get confused -- and where VA often gets it wrong.

> [!important] TDIU doesn't mean you **can't work at all**. It means your service-connected disabilities prevent you from earning above the **federal poverty threshold** through consistent employment.

Under `38 CFR 4.16(a)`, **"marginal employment shall not be considered substantially gainful employment."** Marginal employment exists when:
- Your earned annual income **doesn't exceed the poverty threshold** for one person
- You're employed in a **protected environment** (facts-found basis)

**Protected environment examples:**
- A **family business** with special accommodations
- A **sheltered workshop**
- A job where your employer makes **special accommodations** beyond what's normal

> [!tip] Working part-time, earning below the poverty threshold, or working in a protected environment **does NOT disqualify you** from TDIU. VA must consider the **nature of the employment** and the **reason for any termination**.

> [!warning] VA sometimes denies TDIU simply because a veteran has a job. That's wrong. The question is whether the employment is **substantially gainful** -- not whether it exists at all.

sections.heading: :tools: Key Evidence for Your TDIU Claim
sections.body:
File `VA Form 21-8940` (Application for Increased Compensation Based on Unemployability) and **build your evidence around these five pillars**:

**1. Employment History**
Document job losses, reduced hours, missed days, performance issues, and terminations due to your disabilities. Get **statements from former employers** about accommodations they provided or reasons they let you go.

**2. Functional Limitations**
Medical records documenting what you **physically and mentally cannot do**. Be specific: "cannot sit for more than 30 minutes," "cannot concentrate for sustained periods," "misses 4+ days per month due to flare-ups."

**3. Vocational Expert Opinion**
A vocational rehabilitation specialist can assess your education, training, work history, and functional limitations to opine on your employability. **This is often the strongest single piece of evidence.**

**4. Lay Statements**
Buddy statements from family, former coworkers, and friends who can describe how your disabilities affect your daily functional capacity and ability to maintain employment.

**5. VA and Private Treatment Records**
Ongoing treatment records showing the **severity and persistence** of your conditions.

> [!tip] The vocational expert opinion is the game-changer. If you can afford one, get one. It translates your medical conditions into **employability language** that VA raters understand.

> [!check] Don't file `21-8940` alone with no supporting evidence. Build your package with ALL five evidence types before submitting.

sections.heading: :target: TDIU with a Single Disability vs. Combined
sections.body:
Your strategy changes depending on whether you're claiming TDIU based on **one disability or a combination**.

**Single disability at 60%+:**
- Most straightforward path
- Show that **this one condition alone** prevents you from working
- Focus ALL employment and functional evidence on the impact of this specific disability

**Combined 70% with one at 40%+:**
- Argue the **combined effect** of your service-connected disabilities prevents employment
- Present evidence showing how **multiple conditions interact**

> [!quote] Example: PTSD causes sleep deprivation, which worsens chronic pain, which limits physical capacity, which triggers anxiety. The synergistic effect of all conditions together makes employment impossible, even though no single condition alone would.

> [!important] Remember the **grouping rules** under `38 CFR 4.16(a)`: disabilities from common etiology, a single body system, or affecting paired extremities count as "one disability" for threshold purposes. This can help you meet the 40% or 60% single-disability requirement even when individual ratings are lower.

> [!tip] When arguing combined effect, paint the picture of a **full day in your life**. Show how conditions cascade and compound each other from morning to night.

sections.heading: :link: TDIU and Social Security Disability
sections.body:
VA disability and Social Security Disability (SSDI/SSI) are **separate programs with different standards** -- but they can powerfully reinforce each other.

| Factor | VA TDIU | Social Security |
|--------|---------|----------------|
| Standard | Can't secure/follow substantially gainful employment | Can't engage in substantial gainful activity |
| Based on | Service-connected disabilities only | All disabilities |
| Binding on each other? | No | No |
| Can receive both? | **Yes** | **Yes** |

> [!important] An SSA finding of disability is **not binding on VA**, and VA cannot deny TDIU solely because you don't receive Social Security disability. However, an SSA disability determination is **relevant evidence that VA must consider**.

> [!tip] If you're receiving SSDI, submit your **SSA disability determination letter**, **functional capacity evaluation**, and any **vocational assessments** from your SSA case as evidence in your VA TDIU claim. Another federal agency agreeing you can't work is powerful.

> [!check] You can receive **both** VA disability compensation (including TDIU) and Social Security disability simultaneously. They are **not offset** against each other. Apply for both.

sections.heading: :gavel: Euzebio v. McDonough: A Game-Changing CAVC Decision
sections.body:
This case changed the TDIU landscape. If you've been denied TDIU, you need to know about it.

The CAVC's **Euzebio decision** (Case No. 17-2879) strengthened veterans' rights on TDIU claims by requiring VA to consider **ALL evidence of unemployability** -- not just a single C&P exam.

> [!warning] Before Euzebio, VA would sometimes deny TDIU based primarily on **one C&P examiner's opinion** that the veteran could work, while ignoring lay statements, employment history, vocational opinions, and treatment records that supported unemployability.

> [!important] After Euzebio, VA must weigh the **totality of the evidence**. A single C&P exam opinion cannot override a mountain of other evidence showing unemployability.

> [!check] If you've been denied TDIU based primarily on one C&P exam while other evidence supports unemployability, **cite Euzebio specifically** in your appeal brief or hearing testimony. This case gives you strong grounds for appeal.

sections.heading: :eye: Protected Work and Marginal Employment
sections.body:
Currently working? **That doesn't automatically kill your TDIU claim.** VA must look beyond the mere fact of employment.

**Protected employment situations that may still qualify for TDIU:**
- Working for a **family business** with special accommodations (flexible hours, no productivity requirements, modified duties)
- **Sheltered workshop** environments
- **Part-time work** earning below the poverty threshold
- Employment with **extraordinary accommodations** from the employer
- **Self-employment** where you control your schedule around your disabilities

> [!tip] **Document every accommodation you receive.** If your employer lets you come in late after bad nights, take unscheduled breaks, miss days without consequence, or work reduced hours -- get a **written statement from them** describing these accommodations. This evidence shows your employment is "marginal" even if you technically have a job.

> [!quote] Example: Veteran works 15 hours/week at his brother's shop. Brother lets him leave whenever pain flares up, doesn't track hours, and pays him regardless of output. That's protected/marginal employment -- not substantially gainful.

> [!check] If you're working in a protected environment, get your employer to document the accommodations in writing using `VA Form 21-4192`. This form asks employers about accommodations, missed time, and special considerations.

sections.heading: :medal: Filing Strategy and Next Steps
sections.body:
Ready to file? Here's your complete TDIU battle plan.

**The TDIU filing checklist:**

1. **Check eligibility**: Do you meet `38 CFR 4.16(a)` schedular thresholds? If not, you'll need the extraschedular path
2. **File `VA Form 21-8940`** completely -- employment history, education, training, and detailed description of how **each disability** prevents work
3. **File `VA Form 21-4192`** for each employer in the last 5 years (or have your former employers complete it)
4. **Submit medical evidence** documenting functional limitations that prevent employment
5. **Get a vocational expert opinion** if possible -- this is often the strongest single piece of evidence
6. **Submit lay statements** from people who observe your daily limitations

> [!warning] Don't wait for the "perfect" evidence package. If you're eligible and can't work, **file an Intent to File** to lock in your effective date while you gather supporting documentation.

> [!check] If you're rated at 60%+ (single) or 70%+ (combined with one at 40%), and you can't work because of your service-connected conditions, **file for TDIU now**. Every month you delay is a month of 100% compensation you're not receiving.
